title: Egyptian astronomy

infobox.value: started in [prehistoric](/wiki/Prehistory "Prehistory") times, in the [Predynastic Period](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt"). In the 5th millennium BCE, the stone circles at [Nabta Playa](/wiki/Nabta_Playa "Nabta Playa") may have made use of astronomical alignments. By the time the historical [Dynastic Period](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") began in the 3rd millennium BCE, the 365 day period of the [Egyptian calendar](/wiki/Egyptian_calendar "Egyptian calendar") was already in use, and the observation of [stars](/wiki/Star "Star") was important in determining the annual flooding of the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Astronomy in Ancient Egypt

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Astronomical_Ceiling%2C_Tomb_of_Senenmut_MET_DT207429.jpg/250px-Astronomical_Ceiling%2C_Tomb_of_Senenmut_MET_DT207429.jpg)](/wiki/File:Astronomical_Ceiling,_Tomb_of_Senenmut_MET_DT207429.jpg)

[Astronomical ceiling](/wiki/Astronomical_ceiling_of_Senenmut%27s_Tomb "Astronomical ceiling of Senenmut's Tomb") from the [Tomb of Senenmut](/wiki/TT71 "TT71") ([XVIII Dynasty](/wiki/Eighteenth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt"), circa 1479–1458 BCE), discovered in [Thebes](/wiki/Thebes,_Egypt "Thebes, Egypt"), [Upper Egypt](/wiki/Upper_Egypt "Upper Egypt"); facsimile preserved in the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Goddess_Nut_1.JPG/250px-Goddess_Nut_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Goddess_Nut_1.JPG)

The [sky goddess](/wiki/Sky_goddess "Sky goddess") [Nut](/wiki/Nut_(goddess) "Nut (goddess)") and human figures representing stars and constellations from the star chart in the tomb of [Ramses VI](/wiki/Ramses_VI "Ramses VI").

**Egyptian astronomy** started in [prehistoric](/wiki/Prehistory "Prehistory") times, in the [Predynastic Period](/wiki/Prehistoric_Egypt "Prehistoric Egypt"). In the 5th millennium BCE, the stone circles at [Nabta Playa](/wiki/Nabta_Playa "Nabta Playa") may have made use of astronomical alignments. By the time the historical [Dynastic Period](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") began in the 3rd millennium BCE, the 365 day period of the [Egyptian calendar](/wiki/Egyptian_calendar "Egyptian calendar") was already in use, and the observation of [stars](/wiki/Star "Star") was important in determining the annual flooding of the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile").

The [Egyptian pyramids](/wiki/Egyptian_pyramids "Egyptian pyramids") were carefully aligned towards the [pole star](/wiki/Pole_star "Pole star"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and the temple of [Amun-Re](/wiki/Amun "Amun") at [Karnak](/wiki/Karnak "Karnak") was aligned on the rising of the [midwinter](/wiki/Winter_solstice "Winter solstice") Sun.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") played a considerable part in fixing the dates of [religious festivals](/wiki/Religious_festival "Religious festival") and determining the hours of night, and temple [astrologers](/wiki/Astrology "Astrology") were especially adept at watching the stars and observing the [conjunctions](/wiki/Conjunction_(astronomy) "Conjunction (astronomy)") and risings of the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), and [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet"), as well as the [lunar phases](/wiki/Lunar_phase "Lunar phase").

In [Ptolemaic Egypt](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom"), the Egyptian tradition merged with [Greek astronomy](/wiki/Greek_astronomy "Greek astronomy") and [Babylonian astronomy](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy "Babylonian astronomy"), with the city of [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") in [Lower Egypt](/wiki/Lower_Egypt "Lower Egypt") becoming the centre of scientific activity across the [Hellenistic world](/wiki/Hellenistic_world "Hellenistic world"). [Roman Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_(Roman_province) "Egypt (Roman province)") produced the greatest astronomer of the era, [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") (90–168 CE). His works on astronomy, including the [Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest"), became the most influential books in the history of Western astronomy. Following the [Muslim conquest of Egypt](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Egypt "Muslim conquest of Egypt"), the region came to be [dominated by Arabic culture](/wiki/Egypt_in_the_Middle_Ages "Egypt in the Middle Ages") and [Islamic astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy_in_medieval_Islam "Astronomy in medieval Islam").

The astronomer [Ibn Yunus](/wiki/Ibn_Yunus "Ibn Yunus") (c. 950–1009) observed the [Sun's position](/wiki/Position_of_the_Sun "Position of the Sun") for many years using a large [astrolabe](/wiki/Astrolabe "Astrolabe"), and his observations on [eclipses](/wiki/Eclipse "Eclipse") were still used centuries later. In 1006, [Ali ibn Ridwan](/wiki/Ali_ibn_Ridwan "Ali ibn Ridwan") observed the [SN 1006](/wiki/SN_1006 "SN 1006"), a [supernova](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova") regarded as the brightest stellar event in [recorded history](/wiki/Recorded_history "Recorded history"), and left the most detailed description of it. In the 14th century, [Najm al-Din al-Misri](/wiki/Najm_al-Din_al-Misri "Najm al-Din al-Misri") wrote a treatise describing over 100 different types of scientific and astronomical instruments, many of which he invented himself.

## Ancient Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_astronomy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Ancient Egypt")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f1/Nabta.gif)](/wiki/File:Nabta.gif)

Plan of a stone circle at Nabta, Egypt

Egyptian astronomy dates back to prehistoric times. The presence of [stone circles](/wiki/Stone_circles "Stone circles") at [Nabta Playa](/wiki/Nabta_Playa "Nabta Playa") in [Upper Egypt](/wiki/Upper_Egypt "Upper Egypt") from the [5th millennium BCE](/wiki/5th_millennium_BC "5th millennium BC") show the importance of [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") to the religious life of [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), even in the prehistoric period. The annual [flooding of the Nile](/wiki/Flooding_of_the_Nile "Flooding of the Nile") meant that the [heliacal risings](/wiki/Heliacal_risings "Heliacal risings"), or first visible appearances of [stars](/wiki/Star "Star") at [dawn](/wiki/Dawn "Dawn"), were of special interest in determining when this might occur. The 365 day period of the [Egyptian calendar](/wiki/Egyptian_calendar "Egyptian calendar") was already in use at the beginning of [Egyptian history](/wiki/History_of_ancient_Egypt "History of ancient Egypt"). The [constellation](/wiki/Constellation "Constellation") system used among the [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians") also appears to have been essentially of native origin. Archaeological evidence has linked fractal geometry designs among Sub-Saharan African cultures with Egyptian cosmological signs.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The precise orientation of the [Egyptian pyramids](/wiki/Egyptian_pyramid "Egyptian pyramid") serves as a lasting demonstration of the high degree of technical skill attained in the [3rd millennium BCE](/wiki/3rd_millennium_BC "3rd millennium BC"). It has been shown[*[where?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Naming_conventions_(geographic_names) "Wikipedia:Naming conventions (geographic names)")*] the pyramids were aligned towards the [pole star](/wiki/Pole_star "Pole star"), which, because of the [precession of the equinoxes](/wiki/Precession_of_the_equinoxes "Precession of the equinoxes"), was at that time [Thuban](/wiki/Thuban "Thuban"), a faint star in the constellation of [Draco](/wiki/Draco_(constellation) "Draco (constellation)").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Evaluation of the site of the temple of [Amun-Re](/wiki/Amun-Re "Amun-Re") at [Karnak](/wiki/Karnak "Karnak"), taking into account the change over time of the [obliquity of the ecliptic](/wiki/Obliquity_of_the_ecliptic "Obliquity of the ecliptic"), has shown that the Great Temple was aligned on the rising of the [midwinter](/wiki/Winter_solstice "Winter solstice") Sun.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The length of the corridor down which sunlight would travel would have limited illumination at other times of the year.

Astronomy played a considerable part in [religious](/wiki/Religious "Religious") matters for fixing the dates of festivals and determining the hours of the [night](/wiki/Night "Night"). The titles of several temple books are preserved recording the movements and phases of the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") and [stars](/wiki/Star "Star"). The rising of [Sirius](/wiki/Sirius "Sirius") ([Egyptian](/wiki/Egyptian_language "Egyptian language"): [Sopdet](/wiki/Sopdet "Sopdet"), [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"): [Sothis](/wiki/Sothis_(goddess) "Sothis (goddess)")) at the beginning of the inundation was a particularly important point to fix in the yearly calendar.[[5]](#cite_note-EB1911-5) One of the most important Egyptian astronomical texts was the [Book of Nut](/wiki/Book_of_Nut "Book of Nut"), going back to the [Middle Kingdom](/wiki/Middle_Kingdom_of_Egypt "Middle Kingdom of Egypt") or earlier.

For Ancient Egyptians, the death of a king had a strong connection to the stars. They believed once a king was deceased, their soul would rise to the heavens and become a star.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The [Pyramid Texts](/wiki/Pyramid_Texts "Pyramid Texts") describe the king ascending and becoming the [Morning Star](/wiki/Venus "Venus") among the Imperishable Stars of past kings.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### The First Intermediate Period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_astronomy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The First Intermediate Period")]

Beginning with the [9th Dynasty](/wiki/9th_Dynasty "9th Dynasty"), ancient Egyptians produced 'Diagonal star tables', which were usually painted on the inside surface of wooden coffin lids.[[8]](#cite_note-8) This practice continued until the [12th dynasty](/wiki/12th_dynasty "12th dynasty").[[9]](#cite_note-9) These 'Diagonal star tables' or star charts are also known as 'diagonal star clocks'. In the past they have also been known as 'star calendars', or 'decanal clocks'.[[10]](#cite_note-10) These star charts featuring the paintings of Egyptian deities, [decans](/wiki/Decan "Decan"), constellations, and star observations are also found on the ceilings of tombs and temples.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Lepsius-228-III-rams%C3%A8sVI-d%C3%A9tail.jpg/250px-Lepsius-228-III-rams%C3%A8sVI-d%C3%A9tail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lepsius-228-III-rams%C3%A8sVI-d%C3%A9tail.jpg)

'[Star clock](/wiki/Star_clock "Star clock")' method from the tomb of [Rameses VI](/wiki/Rameses_VI "Rameses VI")

From the tables of stars on the ceiling of the tombs of [Rameses VI](/wiki/Rameses_VI "Rameses VI") and [Rameses IX](/wiki/Rameses_IX "Rameses IX") it seems that for fixing the hours of the night a man seated on the ground faced the Astrologer in such a position that the line of observation of the [pole star](/wiki/Pole_star "Pole star") passed over the middle of his head. On the different days of the year each hour was determined by a fixed star [culminating](/wiki/Culmination "Culmination") or nearly culminating in it, and the position of these stars at the time is given in the tables as in the centre, on the left eye, on the right shoulder, etc. According to the texts, in founding or rebuilding temples the north axis was determined by the same apparatus, and we may conclude that it was the usual one for astronomical observations. In careful hands, it might give results of a high degree of accuracy.[[5]](#cite_note-EB1911-5)

[Macrobius Ambrosius Theodosius](/wiki/Macrobius_Ambrosius_Theodosius "Macrobius Ambrosius Theodosius") (*floruit* 395–423 CE) attributed the planetary theory where the [Earth rotates](/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation") on its axis and the interior planets [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)") and [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") revolve [around the Sun](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism") which in turn revolves around the Earth, to the ancient Egyptians. He called it the "Egyptian System", and stated that "it did not escape the skill of the [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians")", though there is no other evidence it was known in ancient Egypt.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12):  512

### Greco-Roman Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_astronomy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Greco-Roman Egypt")]

See also: [Astrology in Hellenistic Egypt](/wiki/Astrology_in_Hellenistic_Egypt "Astrology in Hellenistic Egypt") and [Greek astronomy](/wiki/Greek_astronomy "Greek astronomy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Astrological_Disc_LACMA_M.80.202.226_%281_of_3%29.jpg/250px-Astrological_Disc_LACMA_M.80.202.226_%281_of_3%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Astrological_Disc_LACMA_M.80.202.226_(1_of_3).jpg)

An Egyptian [30th dynasty (Ptolemaic)](/wiki/Thirtieth_Dynasty_of_Egypt "Thirtieth Dynasty of Egypt") [terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta") [astrological](/wiki/Astrology "Astrology") disc at the [Los Angeles County Museum of Art](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County_Museum_of_Art "Los Angeles County Museum of Art").

Writing in the [Roman era](/wiki/Roman_era "Roman era"), [Clement of Alexandria](/wiki/Clement_of_Alexandria "Clement of Alexandria") gives some idea of the importance of astronomical observations to the sacred rites:

> And after the Singer advances the Astrologer (ὡροσκόπος), with a *horologium* (ὡρολόγιον) in his hand, and a *palm* (φοίνιξ), the symbols of astrology. He must know by heart the [Hermetic](/wiki/Hermetism "Hermetism") astrological books, which are four in number. Of these, one is about the arrangement of the fixed stars that are visible; one on the positions of the sun and moon and five planets; one on the conjunctions and phases of the sun and moon; and one concerns their risings.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The astrologer's instruments (*horologium* and *palm*) are a [plumb line](/wiki/Plumb_line "Plumb line") and sighting instrument. They have been identified with two inscribed objects in the [Berlin Museum](/wiki/Egyptian_Museum_of_Berlin "Egyptian Museum of Berlin"); a short handle from which a plumb line was hung, and a palm branch with a sight-slit in the broader end. In the Ancient Egyptian language they were referred to as the *[merkhet](/wiki/Merkhet "Merkhet")* and *bay* respectively. The palm branch was held close to the eye, the plumb line in the other hand – perhaps at arm's length.[[5]](#cite_note-EB1911-5)[[a]](#cite_note-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Dendera_Deckenrelief_08.JPG/500px-Dendera_Deckenrelief_08.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dendera_Deckenrelief_08.JPG)

Astronomical ceiling relief from [Dendera](/wiki/Dendera_Temple_complex "Dendera Temple complex"), Egypt

Following [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great")'s conquests and the foundation of [Ptolemaic Egypt](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Egypt "Ptolemaic Egypt"), the native Egyptian tradition of astronomy had merged with [Greek astronomy](/wiki/Greek_astronomy "Greek astronomy") as well as [Babylonian astronomy](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy "Babylonian astronomy"). The city of [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") in [Lower Egypt](/wiki/Lower_Egypt "Lower Egypt") became the centre of scientific activity throughout the [Hellenistic civilization](/wiki/Hellenistic_civilization "Hellenistic civilization"). The greatest Alexandrian astronomer of this era was the Greek, [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes") (c. 276–195 BCE), who calculated the size of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), providing an estimate for the circumference of the Earth.

Following the [Roman conquest of Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_(Roman_province) "Egypt (Roman province)"), the region once again became the centre of scientific activity throughout the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). The greatest astronomer of this era was the [Hellenic](/wiki/Hellenization "Hellenization") Egyptian, [Claudius Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") (90–168 CE). Originating from the [Thebaid](/wiki/Thebaid "Thebaid") region of [Upper Egypt](/wiki/Upper_Egypt "Upper Egypt"), he worked at Alexandria and wrote works on astronomy including the *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")*, the *Planetary Hypotheses*, and the *[Tetrabiblos](/wiki/Tetrabiblos "Tetrabiblos")*, as well as the *Handy Tables*, the *Canobic Inscription*, and other works unrelated to astronomy.

Ptolemy's *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* (originally titled *The Mathematical Syntaxis*) is one of the most influential books in the history of Western astronomy. In this book, Ptolemy explained how to predict the behavior of the planets with the introduction of a new mathematical idea, the [equant](/wiki/Equant "Equant").

A few mathematicians of [late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity") wrote commentaries on the *Almagest*, including [Pappus of Alexandria](/wiki/Pappus_of_Alexandria "Pappus of Alexandria") as well as [Theon of Alexandria](/wiki/Theon_of_Alexandria "Theon of Alexandria") and his daughter [Hypatia](/wiki/Hypatia "Hypatia"). Ptolemaic astronomy became standard in medieval western European and [Islamic astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy_in_medieval_Islam "Astronomy in medieval Islam") until it was displaced by [Maraghan](/wiki/Maragheh_observatory "Maragheh observatory"), [heliocentric](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism"), and [Tychonic systems](/wiki/Tychonic_system "Tychonic system") by the 16th century.

## Islamic Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Egyptian_astronomy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Islamic Egypt")]

See also: [Astronomy in medieval Islam](/wiki/Astronomy_in_medieval_Islam "Astronomy in medieval Islam")

Following the [Muslim conquest of Egypt](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Egypt "Muslim conquest of Egypt"), The region came to be [dominated by Arabic culture](/wiki/History_of_Arab_Egypt "History of Arab Egypt"). It was ruled by the [Rashidun](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate"), [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") and [Abbasid Caliphates](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate") up until the 10th century, when the [Fatimids](/wiki/Fatimid_Caliphate "Fatimid Caliphate") founded their own [Caliphate](/wiki/Caliphate "Caliphate") centred around the city of [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo") in Egypt. The region once again became a centre of scientific activity, competing with [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad") for intellectual dominance in the [medieval Islamic world](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age"). By the 13th century, the city of Cairo eventually overtook Baghdad as the intellectual center of the Islamic world.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Ibn Yunus](/wiki/Ibn_Yunus "Ibn Yunus") (c. 950–1009) observed more than 10,000 entries for the Sun's position for many years using a large [astrolabe](/wiki/Astrolabe "Astrolabe") with a diameter of nearly 1.4 meters. His observations on [eclipses](/wiki/Eclipse "Eclipse") were still used centuries later in [Simon Newcomb](/wiki/Simon_Newcomb "Simon Newcomb")'s investigations on the motion of the Moon, while his other observations inspired [Laplace](/wiki/Laplace "Laplace")'s *Obliquity of the Ecliptic* and *Inequalities of Jupiter and Saturn*.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify") (not the title of any work by Laplace)*][*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1006, [Ali ibn Ridwan](/wiki/Ali_ibn_Ridwan "Ali ibn Ridwan") observed the [supernova of 1006](/wiki/SN_1006 "SN 1006"), regarded as the brightest stellar event in recorded history, and left the most detailed description of the temporary star. He says that the object was two to three times as large as the disc of [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") and about one-quarter the brightness of the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), and that the star was low on the southern horizon.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

The [astrolabic](/wiki/Astrolabe "Astrolabe") [quadrant](/wiki/Quadrant_(instrument) "Quadrant (instrument)") was invented in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") in the 11th century or 12th century, and later known in Europe as the "[Quadrans Vetus](/wiki/Quadrans_Vetus "Quadrans Vetus")" (Old Quadrant).[[16]](#cite_note-17):  333 In 14th century [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), Najm al-Din al-Misri (c. 1325) wrote a treatise describing over 100 different types of scientific and astronomical instruments, many of which he invented himself.[[17]](#cite_note-King-18)
